content:
Kosher chocolate, rogue AI, and one extremely unhinged laptop

6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Generated imageI had a really weird day yesterday. Firstly, I got to the office an hour early because I thought I left home at 6.15am, but apparently it was 5.15am. That probably explained why the road was emptier than usual. But at least I had time to eat breakfast. I went to the 24/7 convenience store located in my office’s lobby to look for things to eat. As I was enjoying breakfast, my work friend arrived and decided to get breakfast as well. She had also arrived early because her uncle, the person who drives her to work, needed to get to his office earlier than usual.
As she ate her “expensive chocolate bar,” she realized it had an ‘imported item’ label, which apparently explained the price. I don’t normally think too much about food prices. I already spend a lot of energy working, so I let myself spend a little more on food. I also didn’t know when stores around here started placing the ‘imported item’ label on food, but that’s not important. I don’t pay attention to labels as well.
She started panicking because she couldn’t find the halal certification label on the wrapper, something she said she forgot to check before purchasing. I took a look at the wrapper just to help put her mind at ease, even though it’s not a big deal for me. After looking at the back, I told her it’s fine because there was a kosher certification logo. Over here, the ‘general consensus’ is that if something is kosher, it’s halal as well, so she immediately relaxed after doing a little bit of googling to confirm the logo and enjoyed the rest of her breakfast.
(As I typed this, I had a sudden urge to add a disclaimer. This isn’t religious advice, obviously, so if it’s important to you, definitely ask an expert.)
As she enjoyed her breakfast, she asked me how I knew about kosher certifications, since it is not something people talk about over here. I told her I like to learn about stuff, and apparently that’s a good enough answer. In truth, Instagram and YouTube have been showing me a lot of videos on Judaism recently. I’m not complaining, though. I really do like learning about it.
When I finally entered the office, I checked my work email and found out that I would have to go to the HQ to pick up my laptop replacement. The hinge on my work laptop is broken, and after raising a ticket a few days ago, they finally agreed to give me a replacement before the laptop turned into a detachable. I pinged the morning shift team lead to confirm whether I really needed to go to HQ to collect my replacement. She said that, unfortunately, it’s true.
I googled the location of HQ, and it’s all the way to the other end of the city. Apparently, my company just had to choose the farthest possible location. After a little bit of discussion with the lead, we agreed that I would take the laptop the next day during my day off, and when I returned to work, I would be given an ‘extra’ break because they didn’t want to accidentally count me taking the laptop as doing ‘overtime’. I just love the outsourced employee nonsense sometimes.
When I started doing my actual work, the work AI routed a ticket to me and wrote ‘potential admin error’ in the history notes. I took a look and found the error, but it wasn’t worth the hassle, so I sent the ticket into the completed void. Apparently, the AI disagreed and routed the ticket to someone at an even higher tier. She was shocked and said, “Wait, how did this get to me?” and “Why did it say routing to higher tier for further checking?”
Since I was sitting in front of her, I told her I had already sent it to the void, so that wasn’t possible. But we both knew that ‘impossible’ and ‘not supposed to do that’ weren’t in its vocabulary. Everyone just accepts it. Tech bros on Twitter are arguing about which company has the ‘better’ model. They are all wrong. I know the answer.
At lunch, my work friend and I decided to go to the restaurant. When the waiter asked me what I wanted, I forgot the name of the dish, so I said, “Give me fried rice. The one with the chicken,” instead of “Chicken Fried Rice.” He definitely looked at me like I had lost my mind. I don’t blame him because I’ve just been tired from working all week, so I definitely had lost my mind.
When it came time to go back home, I had to carry my laptop in a semi-open position because closing the lid risks turning it into a two-piece. I definitely looked unhinged standing in the lobby cradling my work laptop like a baby while waiting for my ride. When I reached my condo and got into the elevator, someone remarked that I must be busy because I was ‘still working’. I didn’t say anything, because I didn’t like him very much. He likes to start drama in the condo residence group chat, and I’ve spoken so much the whole day that I reached my daily limit.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Pic of said ‘unhinged’ laptopAt night, I was reminded that I had signed up for a ‘hackathon’ and needed to attend the first ‘seminar’ via Zoom. I don’t remember when I had signed up for it, and I’m not usually the type of person to sign up for things, but since it was my off day the next day anyway, I decided that I might as well attend for the cert. The instructor was yapping nonstop, and the screen was filled with emojis because there were close to ten thousand participants.
He was trying to show how good the model was at creating a working website even on the free account, but it took him several prompts, while mine was done in one prompt, so maybe I should’ve run the session instead and be done in 15 minutes. It was so obvious that they were using the session to get more paid subscribers. Unfortunately for them, I’m stingy when it comes to that. I do still have to submit a ‘working website’ in a week to get the other cert, so I guess I would just submit what the model gave me and collect it.
Earlier today, I woke up a little early to go to HQ to exchange the laptop. When the guard asked me what I wanted to do, I lost my words again and said, “I’m here to give you this laptop, take the new one and leave”. The journey to HQ took 40 minutes, but it took me probably 15 minutes at most to get my laptop. It would have taken me faster if I hadn’t gotten confused looking for the correct floor to press in the elevator.
In the end, yesterday was definitely a bit much, even by my work standards. I accidentally arrived an hour early, got overruled by an AI, forgot the phrase “Chicken fried rice”, carried an unhinged laptop all the way back home, and somehow ended the day with a hackathon I didn’t remember joining. Sometimes life becomes rather strange, and you just keep moving.
